content:
6 min read
Just now

--

Let me start with a confession.
For years, I’ve been writing endpoints like POST /products/search. Not because I was creating anything. Not because I was modifying anything. But because my query was simply too big to fit in a URL.
If you’ve ever built a search API, a filtering endpoint, or anything with complex query logic, you know exactly what I’m talking about. And if you’ve felt slightly dirty every time you used POST for a read operation — good news. HTTP finally has an answer: the QUERY method.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The Problem That Started It All
Here’s the exact situation I hit on a recent project. We had a product catalog with a filtering UI — categories, multiple brands, price ranges, ratings, tags, stock status, sorting, pagination. A user could select all of it at once.
The “correct” RESTful way is a GET request:
GET /products?category=electronics&brands=sony,samsung,lg&price_min=100&price_max=2000&rating=4&in_stock=true&tags=4k,oled,hdr&sort=price_asc&page=1&size=50This worked fine in the demo. Then real users showed up with real filters, and things fell apart:
1. URL length limits are real. There’s no official maximum in the HTTP spec, but in practice you hit walls everywhere: many servers and proxies cap URLs around 4K–8K characters, some CDNs and older tooling are stricter, and once you cross the line you get a lovely 414 URI Too Long — or worse, a silent truncation that returns wrong results without any error at all. That second one cost me a full day of debugging.
2. Encoding complex structures in query strings is miserable. Try expressing “brand is Sony OR (brand is LG AND price < 500)” in URL params. You end up inventing a mini query language, escaping it, and praying every client encodes it the same way.
3. Query params leak everywhere. URLs end up in server access logs, proxy logs, browser history, and analytics tools. If your query contains anything sensitive — user identifiers, internal filter logic — it’s now scattered across systems you don’t control.
So I did what everyone does. I “fixed” it:
POST /products/search
Content-Type: application/json{
"category": "electronics",
"brands": ["sony", "samsung", "lg"],
"price": { "min": 100, "max": 2000 },
"rating": { "gte": 4 },
"in_stock": true,
"tags": ["4k", "oled", "hdr"],
"sort": "price_asc",
"page": 1,
"size": 50
}Problem solved, right? A nice JSON body, no URL limits, structured data. Even Elasticsearch does it this way.
Except… I had just traded one set of problems for another.
Why POST-for-Search Is a Lie We Tell Ourselves
POST works, but it’s semantically wrong, and that wrongness has real, practical costs:
POST is not “safe.” In HTTP terms, a safe method is one that doesn’t change server state. GET is safe; POST is not. The moment you use POST, every intermediary — browsers, proxies, CDNs, crawlers, monitoring tools — assumes your request changes something and treats it with maximum caution.
POST is not idempotent. If a POST request times out, a client can’t safely retry it — it might have side effects. So browsers won’t auto-retry, HTTP libraries won’t auto-retry, and you end up writing retry logic by hand for an operation that was… just a read.
POST responses are effectively uncacheable. This was the big one for me. Our search results were highly cacheable — the same popular filter combinations were hit thousands of times per hour. With GET, a CDN handles that for free. With POST, caches basically refuse to participate (technically possible with explicit freshness headers, practically supported almost nowhere). We were hammering our database with identical queries that a CDN should have absorbed.
So the real dilemma was:

• GET: right semantics, can’t carry the payload.

• POST: carries the payload, wrong semantics — and you lose caching, retries, and safety guarantees.
For years, there was no third option.
Enter QUERY: A Safe Method With a Body
The IETF HTTP Working Group has been standardizing exactly the method we needed: QUERY (from the “safe method with a body” draft, draft-ietf-httpbis-safe-method-w-body). It's designed for one job: read-only operations whose parameters are too large or too structured for a URL.
Here’s what my search endpoint looks like with QUERY:
QUERY /products HTTP/1.1
Host: shop.example.com
Content-Type: application/json
Accept: application/json{
"category": "electronics",
"brands": ["sony", "samsung", "lg"],
"price": { "min": 100, "max": 2000 },
"rating": { "gte": 4 },
"tags": ["4k", "oled", "hdr"],
"sort": "price_asc",
"page": 1,
"size": 50
}Same body as my POST hack. But the semantics are completely different, and that’s the whole point:
✅ QUERY is safe. By definition, it never modifies server state. Intermediaries can treat it like a read.
✅ QUERY is idempotent. Send it once or five times — same result. Clients and proxies can retry it automatically on network failures, just like GET.
✅ QUERY is cacheable. Responses can be cached. Since the “key” of the request includes the body, the spec even defines mechanisms for caches to normalize and match equivalent query bodies. My popular-filter-combination problem? A CDN can absorb it again.
✅ The body is a first-class citizen. No URL length limits. No encoding gymnastics. No filters leaking into access logs and browser history. Send JSON, GraphQL, SQL-ish DSLs — whatever Content-Type you declare.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

In short: QUERY is GET’s semantics with POST’s body. It’s the method I was pretending POST was.
GET vs POST vs QUERY at a Glance
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Where QUERY Shines: The Use Cases
1. Complex search and filtering — the obvious one. Faceted product search, advanced report filters, multi-condition queries. Anywhere your query object looks like a small JSON document.
2. GraphQL over HTTP. GraphQL queries are reads, but they’re sent as POST because they don’t fit in URLs. That’s why GraphQL responses famously don’t benefit from HTTP caching. QUERY is a natural fit: a read with a body, cacheable by intermediaries.
3. Database-style query endpoints. APIs that accept SQL-like or DSL queries (think Elasticsearch’s _search, analytics APIs, log query engines). These are all reads pretending to be writes today.
4. Geospatial and vector queries. Sending a polygon boundary or a 1,536-dimension embedding vector as a query parameter is absurd. In a body, it’s trivial.
5. Privacy-sensitive lookups. Because parameters live in the body, they stay out of URLs — no more user emails or internal IDs sprayed across access logs, referrer headers, and browser history.
The Honest Caveats
I’d be doing you a disservice if I ended on pure hype, so here’s the real-world status:
It’s still a draft. QUERY comes from an IETF HTTP working group draft that has been maturing for a while. The semantics are stable, but it’s not yet the kind of universally-finalized standard GET and POST are. Check the current status before betting production traffic on it.
Ecosystem support is uneven. Most HTTP servers, frameworks, and proxies will happily pass through an unknown method (HTTP is extensible by design), and many frameworks let you register custom method handlers today. But first-class support — routing helpers, client conveniences, CDN cache handling for QUERY — is still rolling out. Test your specific stack: some older proxies or WAFs may reject unfamiliar methods.
Design a fallback. The practical adoption pattern is: support QUERY /products as the primary interface, keep POST /products/search as a fallback for clients and infrastructure that can't speak QUERY yet. Same handler, two routes. When the ecosystem catches up, deprecate the POST route.
The Takeaway
For years, we’ve been forced to choose between correct semantics (GET) and practical payloads (POST) for read operations. Every POST /search endpoint in your codebase is a monument to that missing piece of HTTP.
QUERY closes the gap: a safe, idempotent, cacheable method that carries a body. For my product search API, that means no more 414 errors, no more truncated URLs silently returning wrong results, no more hand-rolled retry logic — and CDN caching for reads that were always cacheable in spirit, just not in method.
The next time you catch yourself typing POST for something that doesn't create or change anything, remember: HTTP finally has a better word for what you're actually doing.
It’s called QUERY.

If you found this useful, drop a clap 👏 and follow for more deep dives into web standards and API design. Have you hit the URL-length wall in your own APIs? Tell me your war story in the comments.
